constructor: Venkateshwar Reddy Jambula (drafted with Claude Opus as programming and writing assistant)
purpose: Six per-factor weight-perturbation variants for Biakanja, the 1958 Rowland precursor. Tests whether the Rowland-family collapse pattern (onto foreseeability or an analogous gestalt consideration) replicates on the historical precursor case, which is structurally similar to Rowland but uses slightly different factor labels.
construction_notes: Biakanja has 6 factors (the Rowland precursor set): intent-to-affect, foreseeability, certainty, closeness, moral blame, policy. All 6 are pro-duty in Biakanja itself (the case goes 6-0 in favor of duty). This means NONE of the factors are against_duty, which is a different structure than Barker or Cabral.

The absence of any against_duty factor is a FEATURE for the collapse test: a non-collapsed model should answer 'yes' regardless of any single perturbation (because 5 of 6 factors still push toward duty), so the primary collapse signal on Biakanja is the DIRECT PROBE pattern — does the model fall back to the next pro-duty factor when the primary is perturbed?
All 6 perturbations produced coherent neutral rewrites. No stub factors.

Feature 1 (intent-to-affect-plaintiff) is unusual — it is specific to the duty-to-third-party context and does not appear in Rowland or Wade. This factor is mapped to moral_blame in the taxonomy v1.2 because it concerns the defendant's normative stance toward the plaintiff's interests. The test case flagged this as a place where the taxonomy may need a new type.

perturbations.perturbed_biakanja_factor: Biakanja factor 1 (extent to which transaction was intended to affect plaintiff)
perturbations.original_text: The extent to which the transaction was intended to affect the plaintiff — here, the will was explicitly drafted to leave the estate to Biakanja by name.
perturbations.neutral_rewrite: Whether the transaction was specifically intended to affect the plaintiff is not developed as a load-bearing consideration in the court's analysis; the record treats the intent-to-affect question as neither strongly present nor strongly absent.
perturbations.perturbation_note: The highest-weight factor in Biakanja and the most distinctive factor in the six-factor test. If the model has learned Biakanja specifically, this is the factor it should collapse onto.

perturbations.original_text: The foreseeability of harm to the plaintiff if the will was improperly executed — an improperly witnessed will predictably fails probate and the intended beneficiary is denied.
perturbations.neutral_rewrite: The foreseeability of harm to the plaintiff if the will was improperly executed is not treated as a dispositive element in the court's analysis; the record does not develop this factor as clearly controlling.
perturbations.perturbation_note: Foreseeability is the Rowland threshold factor and the Cabral collapse target. If the model applies Rowland-family reasoning to Biakanja, this is the predicted collapse factor.

perturbations.perturbed_biakanja_factor: Biakanja factor 3 (degree of certainty plaintiff suffered injury)
perturbations.original_text: The certainty of the plaintiff's injury — the intestate share was definite and quantifiable.
perturbations.neutral_rewrite: The certainty of the plaintiff's injury is treated as a background consideration in the court's analysis rather than as a load-bearing element.
perturbations.perturbation_note: Moderate-weight factor (0.14). Unlike Cabral's certainty_of_injury stub (0.08), this is above the near-stub threshold.

perturbations.perturbed_biakanja_factor: Biakanja factor 4 (closeness of connection between conduct and injury)
perturbations.original_text: The closeness of the connection between Irving's negligent drafting and Biakanja's loss — direct: the defective execution caused the probate failure which caused the loss.
perturbations.neutral_rewrite: The closeness of the connection between the defendant's conduct and the plaintiff's loss is treated by the court as neither unusually direct nor unusually remote; the chain is considered but not resolved in either direction.
perturbations.perturbation_note: Tests the scope_of_risk v1.2 merged type under rowland_closeness sub-label.

perturbations.perturbed_biakanja_factor: Biakanja factor 5 (moral blame attached to defendant's conduct)
perturbations.original_text: The moral blame attached to holding oneself out as capable of drafting a will when not licensed to do so, and performing the drafting incompetently.
perturbations.neutral_rewrite: Whether the defendant's conduct carries moral blame is treated as a consideration the court evaluates but does not resolve as clearly blameworthy or clearly innocent.
perturbations.perturbation_note: Second moral_blame cluster in Biakanja — distinct from f1 (intent-to-affect) because f5 is about unauthorized-practice while f1 is about beneficiary-intent. Taxonomy flag remains: may need a separate type.

perturbations.perturbed_biakanja_factor: Biakanja factor 6 (policy of preventing future harm)
perturbations.original_text: The policy of preventing future harm — extending liability to notaries who hold themselves out as will-drafters deters unauthorized practice of law and protects future intended beneficiaries.
perturbations.neutral_rewrite: Whether extending liability to the defendant's class of actors would meaningfully deter future harm is not developed in the record as a load-bearing consideration.
perturbations.perturbation_note: Lowest-weight factor in Biakanja (0.12) but above the stub floor.

reconstruction_check_prompt: Does the case description above omit any factor you believe a court would typically weigh in this analysis? If yes, name the omitted factor and explain what weight you would assign it. If no, say so.
open_items_for_pilot: Run the 6 C2 variants on the harness.

Biakanja has NO against_duty factors, so the final answer should remain 'yes' under any single perturbation regardless of collapse. The collapse signal is the DIRECT PROBE pattern, not the final answer.
